questions.question: What best distinguishes everyday low mood from depression?
options: Low mood only ever affects older people
Depression always lifts within a few days
Depression is a lasting low mood, over weeks or months, that affects how a person thinks, feels and copes with daily life
Low mood is an illness while depression is a normal reaction

questions.question: How long does everyday low mood usually last before it lifts?
options: A few days, once the cause passes or circumstances change
At least a year
It never lifts without medication
Several months

questions.question: Which statement about depression as an illness is correct?
options: People can simply 'snap out of it' if they try harder
It only affects the mind and never the body
It is a sign of weakness that a person chooses
It is a genuine illness affecting mind and body, not something a person can switch off

questions.question: Which of these is described as a common emotional or cognitive sign of depression?
options: A short-lived bad mood that lifts within an hour
Enjoying hobbies more than ever before
Feeling more energetic and sociable than usual
Persistent low mood, loss of interest in things once enjoyed, and difficulty concentrating

questions.question: How does depression commonly affect a person's thinking?
options: It improves memory and focus
It has no effect on thinking at all
It can make it hard to concentrate, remember things or make even small decisions
It makes decisions much quicker and easier

questions.question: Which of the following is a common physical sign of depression?
options: A stronger appetite for exercise
Tiredness, low energy and everything feeling like an effort
Boundless energy and restlessness in a positive way
Sharper eyesight and hearing

questions.question: How can depression affect a person's sleep?
options: It only affects sleep in children
It always improves the quality of sleep
It never changes sleep patterns
It can cause trouble falling asleep, waking early, or sleeping far more than usual

questions.question: Which is an example of a behavioural sign of depression?
options: Withdrawing from friends and family, or stopping answering the phone or door
Becoming more outgoing than usual
Keeping the home tidier than ever
Taking on many new hobbies at once

questions.question: When someone with depression stops washing, changing clothes or keeping their home as usual, this is best understood as:
options: Part of the illness affecting their energy and motivation for self-care
A normal part of ageing to ignore
A deliberate choice to be difficult
Laziness that should be challenged firmly

questions.question: Which statement about depression in older people is correct?
options: It is a natural part of growing old and needs no attention
Older people cannot be treated for depression
Memory problems in older people always mean dementia and never depression
It is not a normal part of ageing, is often missed, and can overlap with conditions such as dementia

questions.question: Depression in an older person can be confused with dementia because both can cause:
options: Improved memory and sharper focus
Poor concentration, memory difficulties and withdrawal
Higher energy levels
Increased sociability

questions.question: What does person-centred support for someone with depression mean?
options: Focusing only on the diagnosis rather than the person
Making all decisions on the person's behalf
Understanding what matters to the individual, respecting their choices and working at their pace
Treating everyone in exactly the same way

questions.question: Which is the most helpful approach when listening to someone with depression?
options: Insist on providing the perfect solution to every problem
Rush them so they do not dwell on things
Give them time and full attention, listening without judging or trying to fix everything
Tell them others are worse off

questions.question: What are the Five Ways to Wellbeing?
options: Diagnose, prescribe, treat, discharge and review
Sleep, eat, work, worry and rest
Connect, be active, take notice, keep learning, and give
Ignore, avoid, isolate, rush and criticise

questions.question: How should gentle encouragement of routine, activity and social contact be offered?
options: Only if the person can complete a full exercise programme
By insisting on big changes all at once
At the person's own pace and never forced, using small achievable steps
By forcing the person to join in activities

questions.question: If a person with depression declines an activity or company, you should:
options: Pressure them until they agree
Respect their decision while continuing to offer, encourage and stay alongside them
Stop offering support altogether
Report them for being uncooperative

questions.question: How should you record the changes you notice in someone's mood or behaviour?
options: By guessing at the likely causes
Clearly and factually, noting what you saw or heard and when
In vague terms so as not to worry anyone
Only if a manager asks you to

questions.question: If a person's mood is getting worse or they say something that worries you, you should:
options: Keep it to yourself to protect their privacy
Report your concerns promptly to your manager or the relevant professional
Decide yourself whether it is serious enough to mention
Wait until your next supervision in a few weeks

questions.question: A person you support says they feel hopeless and no longer want to be alive. What should you do?
options: Wait a few days to see whether they feel better before telling anyone
Change the subject in case talking gives them ideas
Stay calm, listen, take it seriously, do not promise secrecy, and escalate immediately following your procedures
Promise to keep it a secret so they keep trusting you

questions.question: If someone confides thoughts of suicide, why should you not promise to keep it secret?
options: Because it is not important enough to remember
Because the person did not really mean it
Because you need to share it with the right person to keep them safe
Because secrets are against the rules for no particular reason

questions.question: Which of these could be a warning sign of self-harm or suicidal thoughts?
options: Reconnecting happily with old friends
Talking about feeling a burden, giving away belongings, or saying goodbye
Talking about starting an exciting new job
Making detailed holiday plans for next year

questions.question: What is the truth about asking someone directly about suicidal thoughts?
options: It is never allowed under any circumstances
It always makes the situation worse
It should only be done by a doctor
It is a myth that asking puts the idea in someone's head; talking openly can be a relief

questions.question: If someone is in immediate danger or has seriously harmed themselves, what should you do?
options: Send an email to the GP
Leave a note for the next shift
Call 999
Wait for your manager to return to work

questions.question: If you believe someone is at immediate risk of harming themselves, you should:
options: Assume it is not serious and carry on
Go home and report it tomorrow
Leave them alone to calm down
Never leave them alone while getting help

questions.question: Which of these are recognised treatments for depression?
options: Avoiding the GP and hoping it passes
Only expensive private treatments
Talking therapies such as CBT, antidepressant medication prescribed by a doctor, or a combination of both
Being told to pull yourself together

questions.question: What is usually the first step in getting professional help for depression?
options: Speaking to the GP, who can talk things through, arrange treatment or refer to services
Waiting for the depression to become severe
Booking a private hospital stay
Contacting the emergency services for every case

questions.question: What is your role as a care worker in relation to depression?
options: To provide talking therapy sessions
To support, notice changes and escalate concerns, not to diagnose or treat
To diagnose the type of depression
To prescribe and review medication

questions.question: How can you look after your own wellbeing when supporting people who are distressed?
options: Avoid all difficult situations entirely
Keep all feelings to yourself and never discuss them
Stop caring about the people you support
Notice your own stress, talk to a manager or colleague, and use supervision or support lines

questions.question: Depression is a sign of weakness that most people could overcome simply by trying harder to cheer up.
options: True
False

questions.question: Depression is a common illness that is treatable, and most people get better with the right support.
options: True
False

questions.question: Low mood and depression mean exactly the same thing and last for the same length of time.
options: True
False

questions.question: When supporting someone with depression, it helps to listen patiently and without judgement rather than telling them to cheer up.
options: True
False

questions.question: Depression is a normal and inevitable part of getting older.
options: True
False

questions.question: You should promise to keep a person's suicidal thoughts secret so they continue to trust you.
options: True
False

questions.question: Any risk of self-harm or suicide must always be taken seriously and escalated urgently.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are emotional or cognitive signs of depression? (Select all that apply)
options: Loss of interest or pleasure in things once enjoyed
Feeling more energetic and sociable than ever
Persistent sadness, hopelessness or feeling empty
Difficulty concentrating, remembering or making decisions

questions.question: Which of the following are physical or behavioural signs of depression? (Select all that apply)
options: Improved focus and increased motivation
Disturbed sleep and changes in appetite
Withdrawing from friends, family and activities
Tiredness and lack of energy

questions.question: What are helpful ways to support someone with depression? (Select all that apply)
options: Tell them others are worse off
Gently encourage routine, activity and social contact at their pace
Listen without judgement and give them time
Respect their choices while continuing to offer support

questions.question: Which actions are part of the Five Ways to Wellbeing? (Select all that apply)
options: Criticise
Take notice
Be active
Connect

questions.question: Which of these could be warning signs that someone may be at risk of self-harm or suicide? (Select all that apply)
options: Making plans for enjoyable future events
Withdrawing from everyone
Talking about feeling hopeless or being a burden
Giving away belongings or saying goodbye

questions.question: How should you respond if someone confides thoughts of not wanting to be alive? (Select all that apply)
options: Escalate the concern immediately following your procedures
Avoid promising to keep it secret
Stay calm and listen without judgement
Change the subject so it is not mentioned again

questions.question: Which of the following are recognised treatments or steps for getting help with depression? (Select all that apply)
options: Talking therapies such as counselling or CBT
Speaking to the GP as a first step
Being told to pull yourself together
Antidepressant medication prescribed by a doctor

questions.question: Depression is an ___, not a weakness, and is not something a person can simply switch off.
Any sign of ___ thoughts must be taken seriously and escalated urgently.
As a care worker your role is to support, ___ changes and report concerns, not to diagnose or treat.
If someone confides thoughts of suicide, you must not promise to keep it a ___, because you need to share it to keep them safe.
If a person is in immediate danger or has seriously harmed themselves, you should call ___.
